She had died and left one child.
And Archibald generally submitted, for the force of habit is strong.
His confidential clerk and manager stood near him.
It was mr Dill, a little, meek looking man with a bald head.
"Can I see mr Carlyle?"
mr Dill rose from his seat and shook hands with her.
"Presently, Miss Barbara.
He is engaged just now.
The justices are with him."
He must not see me.
An ominous sound of talking; the justices were evidently coming forth.
"They are gone now, and the coast's clear, Miss Barbara."
mr Dill shut me into his room."
"It would be-it might be-death!"
"The doors are double doors; did you notice that they were?"
"Richard!" repeated mr Carlyle.
"At West Lynne!"
And, oh, Archibald!
He says he is innocent."
"Sit down, Barbara," he said drawing her chair closer.
Barbara sat down again, but her manner was hurried and nervous.
It would look so peculiar to see me here; but mamma was too unwell to come herself-or rather, she feared papa's questioning, if he found out that she came."
"What Thorn?" asked mr Carlyle, suppressing all signs of incredulity.
"I don't know; a friend of Afy's, he said.
You are so clever, you can do anything."
mr Carlyle smiled.
Was this the purport of Richard's visit-to say this?"
"If so, I must send to the bank.
"Not until evening.
Can you manage to see Richard?"
"A farm laborer's, the best he could adopt about here, with large black whiskers.
And now," continued Barbara, "I want you to advise me; had I better inform mamma that Richard is here, or not?"
Would it be advisable to acquaint her?"
"It is only natural.
Can you devise any plan?
He had dropped his eyelids in thought.
"Have you told me all?" he asked presently, lifting them.
"I think so."
"It-it might excite suspicions; some one might see me, too, and mention it to papa.
Neither ought you to send to our house."
"Well-contrive to be in the street at four this afternoon.
Stay, that's your dinner hour; be walking up the street at three, three precisely; I will meet you."
"Why-what on earth!" began she, "have you been with Archibald for?"
Miss Carlyle did not believe a word.
A trifling matter, relating to a little money.
It's nothing, indeed."
A clerk answered it.
"Go to the Buck's Head.
If mr Hare and the other magistrates are there, ask them to step over to me."
Come at seven, not later, and you will find my father's old jar replenished with the best broadcut, and half a dozen churchwarden pipes.
Shall it be so?"
The whole five accepted the invitation eagerly.
Soon after mr Carlyle was left alone another clerk entered.
"Miss Carlyle is asking to see you, sir, and Colonel Bethel's come again."
"Send in Miss Carlyle first," was the answer.
"What is it, Cornelia?"
"Ah!
How can I give my orders?"
"I thought business would have called me out, but I am not going now.
We will dine a little earlier, though, Cornelia, say a quarter before six.
I have invited-"
"What's up, Archibald?" interrupted Miss Carlyle.
"Up!
I am very busy, Cornelia, and Colonel Bethel is waiting; I will talk to you at dinner time.
I have invited a party for to night."
"A party!" echoed Miss Carlyle.
"Four or five of the justices are coming in to smoke their pipes.
"They shan't come!" screamed Miss Carlyle.
"Do you think I'll be poisoned with tobacco smoke from a dozen pipes?"
"Nor they either.
Clean curtains are just put up throughout the house, and I'll have no horrid pipes to blacken them."
"And now, Cornelia, I really must beg you to leave me."
"You are very clever, Archie, but you can't do me.
I ask you: to hear your opinion about the scrape the bench have got into, is yours.
I should like to know what you and Barbara do with a secret between you."
mr Carlyle knew her and her resolute expression well, and he took his course, to tell her the truth.
mr Carlyle bent forward and spoke in a whisper.
Richard Hare has returned."
"Richard Hare!
Is he mad?"
"Is he at their house?"
Out upon you for a bold, bad man!"
Certain it is, that the ominous sound of "Duck him," was breathed forth by a voice, and it was caught up and echoed around.
"Duck him!
Duck him!
The pond be close at hand.
Let's give him a taste of his deservings!
Him put up for others at West Lynne!

They set upon him, twenty pairs of hands at least, strong, rough, determined hands; not to speak of the tagrag's help, who went in with cuffs, and kicks, and pokes, and taunts, and cheers, and a demoniac dance.
They dragged him through a gap in the hedge, a gap that no baby could have got through in a cool moment; but most of us know the difference between coolness and excitement.
The hedge was extensively damaged, but Justice Hare, to whom it belonged, would forgive that.
mr Drake and the lawyer-for the other was a lawyer-were utterly powerless to stop the catastrophe.
Had she interfered for his protection, she could not have been heard; and if she could have been, there's no knowing whether she would have done it.
One pulled him, another pushed him, a third shook him by the collar, half a dozen buffeted him, and all abused him.
"Mercy!
Mercy!" shrieked the victim, his knees bending and his teeth chattering-"a little mercy for the love of Heaven!"
Much he knows of Heaven!"
Out of the pea soup before he was quite dead, quite senseless.
The laborers, their duty done, walked coolly away; the tagrag withdrew to a safe distance, waiting for what might come next; and Miss Carlyle moved away also.
What did she think of his beauty now?
I know what she thought of her past folly.
Miss Carlyle never spoke a word.
"It's very odd," thought Miss Corny.
They were passing a spectacle shop, and Madame Vine had halted at the door, one foot on its step.
Miss Carlyle followed her in.
She put them on, there and then, Miss Carlyle's eyes searching her face inquisitively all the time.
"My eyes are not strong."
But why wear colored glasses?
"I am accustomed to colored ones.
Miss Corny paused.
"Here!
Here!
What's up?
What's this?"
Quite a collection of gentlemen-mr
Sir Francis Levison?
Could it be?
What on earth had put him into that state?
"You see him-my brother Archibald?"
"I see him," faltered Lady Isabel.
"And you see him, that pitiful outcast, who is too contemptible to live?
Look at the two, and contrast them.
Look well."
"Yes!" was the gaping answer.
Did she come to repentance, think you?"
He objected.
He'd have burst with it, if he hadn't, I expect; I never saw a chap so excited.
peter cried."
"Cried?" echoed Miss Carlyle.
"I didn't see her, and I was present."
"What did she go into hysterics for?" again snapped Miss Carlyle.
"It wouldn't have done her harm had they ducked her too," was the angry response.
Joyce was silent.
"Joyce," resumed Miss Carlyle, abruptly changing the subject, "who does the governess put you in mind of?"
"The governess?
Do you mean Madame Vine?"
"I did to day," returned Miss Carlyle.
"And I can tell you, Joyce, that I was confounded at the likeness.
It is an extraordinary likeness.
Miss Carlyle turned to the earl.
"Was it a positively ascertained fact that Lady Isabel died?"
The earl stared with all his might; he thought it the strangest question that ever was asked him.
Died?
Certainly she died."
"When the result of the accident was communicated to you, you made inquiry yourself into its truth, its details, I believe?"
"It was my duty to do so.
There was no one else to undertake it."
"Did you ascertain positively, beyond all doubt, that she did die?"
"Of a surety I did.
She died in the course of the same night.
A pause.
But she returned to the charge, as if difficult to be convinced.
You are sure that she is dead?"
"I am as sure that she is dead as that we are living," decisively replied the earl: and he spoke but according to his belief.
Convincing proofs; and Miss Carlyle lent her ear to them.
She arose; her face burning, her heart throbbing.
"There was no difference," she murmured, and then she took courage and spoke more openly.
"Yes, sir."
